siteDescription: Clinical psychology training rooted in South Central LA. Monthly insights on cultural competence, clinical supervision, and building a forensic psychology practice.
doctorQuote: When I was a grad student, nobody told me how to navigate the real challenges of clinical practice. This is what I wish I'd had.

children.text: I'm a product of South Central Los Angeles, shaped by community resilience and the complexities of serving diverse populations. For over two decades, I've worked at the intersection of clinical psychology, cultural competence, and practical training.
Field Notes is my way of sharing what I've learned — not the sanitized textbook version, but the reality of building a meaningful clinical practice.

heroHeadline: Clinical Psychology Training: The Reality Behind the Practice
heroSubheadline: Monthly field notes on cultural competence, clinical supervision, and building a psychology practice from the ground up — straight from South Central LA.
newsletterTagline: Monthly insights on cultural competence, clinical supervision, and building a psychology practice

name: Marcus Thompson
title: PhD Candidate, Clinical Psychology
quote: Before SCTC, I thought forensic psych was all courtroom drama. Dr. Kennedy showed me the human side — the families, the communities, the cultural nuances that shape every case. That perspective completely changed how I approach my research and clinical work.

name: Aisha Patel
title: Post-Doctoral Fellow
institution: The Chicago School of Professional Psychology
quote: The supervision I received at SCTC was unlike anything else in my training. Dr. Kennedy doesn't just teach you to write competency reports — he teaches you to see the whole person behind the referral question. That's rare, and it made all the difference in my career.

title: Writing Your First Competency Report
excerpt: A step-by-step guide to writing clear, defensible competency evaluation reports — from assessment to final submission.

children.text: Writing Your First Competency Report

Your first competency evaluation report can feel overwhelming. There are legal standards to meet, clinical observations to document, and conclusions that could significantly impact someone's life. This guide walks you through the process step by step.
Understanding the Referral Question

Before you write a single word, make sure you deeply understand what the court is asking. The referral question drives everything — your assessment battery, your clinical interviews, and ultimately your conclusions.
Structuring Your Report

A well-structured report tells a story. It moves logically from background information through assessment findings to clinical conclusions. Each section should build on the previous one, creating a narrative that's both scientifically rigorous and accessible to non-clinical readers.
Cultural Considerations

Every competency evaluation exists within a cultural context. Language barriers, cultural expressions of distress, and socioeconomic factors can all influence your findings. Documenting these considerations isn't optional — it's what separates a competent evaluation from an excellent one.

title: Cultural Formulation in Practice
excerpt: Moving beyond theoretical frameworks to actual clinical application — with case examples from forensic and community settings.

children.text: Cultural Formulation in Practice

Most training programs teach the DSM's Cultural Formulation Interview as a checklist. But real cultural competence goes far beyond checking boxes. It requires genuine curiosity about how culture shapes every aspect of a person's experience — including their experience of being evaluated.
Beyond the Checklist

The Cultural Formulation Interview is a starting point, not an endpoint. Use it as a framework, but let the conversation flow naturally. Your client will tell you what matters most to them if you create space for it.

title: Understanding Training Site Culture
excerpt: Questions to ask during internship interviews that reveal whether a site will truly support your development.

children.text: Understanding Training Site Culture

Not all training sites are created equal. The match process isn't just about getting in — it's about finding the right fit. Here are the questions that will help you evaluate whether a site's culture aligns with your values and growth needs.

title: What Supervisors Actually Look For
excerpt: Beyond the credentials — how to demonstrate clinical judgment in your application materials and supervision sessions.

children.text: What Supervisors Actually Look For

After 24 years of supervising trainees, I can tell you that the students who stand out aren't always the ones with the highest GPAs or the most impressive CVs. What I look for is clinical curiosity, cultural humility, and the willingness to sit with uncertainty.

title: Navigating Your First Year of Supervision
excerpt: What to expect, how to prepare, and how to get the most out of your supervision hours during internship.

children.text: Navigating Your First Year of Supervision

Your first year of supervision sets the foundation for your entire career. It's where you develop your clinical identity, learn to trust your instincts, and — most importantly — learn that not knowing is not the same as being incompetent.

title: The Ethics of Cultural Competence
excerpt: When cultural sensitivity and clinical obligation seem to conflict — real scenarios and how to think through them.

children.text: The Ethics of Cultural Competence

What happens when respecting a client's cultural framework seems to conflict with evidence-based practice? These are the moments that define your growth as a clinician. Let's walk through some real scenarios.

title: Building a Post-Doc Application Strategy
excerpt: Post-doc applications are a different beast. Here's what hiring committees actually weigh when reviewing your materials.

children.text: Building a Post-Doc Application Strategy

The post-doc market is competitive, but strategic applicants consistently outperform those who take a scatter-shot approach. Here's what hiring committees at forensic and community mental health sites are actually looking for.

title: Supervision: What to Expect
description: How supervision works at SCTC — structure, expectations, and growth areas.

children.text: What Clinical Supervision Looks Like at SCTC

Supervision is the backbone of your training experience. At SCTC, we take a developmental approach — meeting you where you are and building from there. Whether you're in your first practicum or preparing for licensure, supervision is tailored to your growth edge.
Structure & Frequency

You'll receive a minimum of two hours of individual supervision per week, plus one hour of group supervision. Individual sessions focus on your active cases, while group sessions cover broader clinical and ethical topics.
Cultural Competence in Supervision
Every supervision session at SCTC incorporates cultural formulation. We don't treat culture as an add-on — it's woven into how we conceptualize cases, build treatment plans, and evaluate progress.

title: Cultural Humility in Clinical Practice
description: A foundational article on moving from cultural competence to cultural humility.

children.text: From Competence to Humility

Cultural competence implies a destination — a point at which you've 'learned enough.' Cultural humility, by contrast, is a lifelong practice of self-reflection and openness. It acknowledges that you will never fully understand another person's cultural experience, and that's okay.
What Cultural Humility Looks Like in Practice

In clinical practice, cultural humility means asking instead of assuming. It means sitting with discomfort when a client's worldview challenges your own. And it means recognizing that your clinical training, however rigorous, was shaped by a particular cultural lens.
